name: Ceramics and Pottery
tagline: Clay shaped and fired into durable vessels, bricks, and refractories — humanity's first high-temperature materials science
problem:
Organic containers rot; stone bowls are heavy and hard to shape; baskets leak. Storing water, fermenting foods, cooking stews, and transporting liquids requires impermeable, heat-tolerant vessels. Sun-dried clay holds shape but dissolves in water. The problem ceramics solve is irreversible hardening: transform mouldable earth into glassy-bonded solids that resist water and heat.

Beyond vessels, fired clay becomes brick — modular construction units that stack into granaries, kilns, and furnaces. Refractory ceramics line smelting furnaces where steel would slump. The same underlying sintering principle scales from cups to city walls.

The difficulty is process control: clay must be worked without laminations that become cracks; drying must be slow enough to avoid differential shrinkage; firing must climb through quartz inversion and sintering ranges without exploding trapped water or carbonaceous cores.
overview:
Pottery begins with clay — sheet silicates with adsorbed water and often fluxing impurities like iron, potassium, and calcium. Temper (sand, crushed shell, grog) is added to reduce shrinkage cracking. Wedging homogenises clay and aligns particles. Throwing, coiling, or pressing forms the piece; leather-hard trimming refines geometry.

Drying removes free water; at the kiln, chemically bound water drives off first — the critical stage where rapid heating causes steam pressure that pops pieces. Carbon burns out next; then vitrification begins as particle surfaces melt slightly and fuse — the boundary between earthenware, stoneware, and porcelain depends on peak temperature, atmosphere, and body recipe.

Glazes are glassy coatings: silica plus fluxes (alkali, alkaline earth) plus stabilisers (alumina) melted onto the body, often coloured by transition metal oxides. Glazes must thermal-expansion-match the body or craze or shiver.

Bricks are low-precision pottery optimised for stacking and thermal mass; refractory bricks use high-alumina or silica recipes to survive furnace linings. All share sintering: solid-state bonding plus partial liquid phase formation at high temperature.

principles.name: Plasticity and particle packing
principles.explanation: Water films allow clay platelets to slide; uniform packing reduces weak planes. Laminations from poor wedging become failure planes after firing.

principles.name: Sintering
principles.explanation: At elevated temperature, diffusion across particle contacts creates strength without full melting — the backbone of pottery and many refractories.

principles.name: Vitrification
principles.explanation: Partial melting forms glassy phase filling pores — increases strength and decreases permeability.

principles.name: Thermal expansion mismatch
principles.explanation: Body and glaze must shrink compatibly on cooling or stresses crack the glaze or body.

components.name: Clay body
components.madeFrom: Clay with temper and possibly grog
components.criticalNote: Test fire small samples when sourcing unknown clays

components.name: Kiln
components.function: Raises ware to sintering temperature in controlled atmosphere
components.position: Updraft, downdraft, or pit configurations historically
components.criticalNote: Reduction versus oxidation changes iron colours and some defect rates

components.name: Saggar (optional)
components.function: Protects ware from flame impingement and ash
components.position: Inside kiln chamber around delicate pieces

rawMaterials.name: Plastic clay
earthLocations: Kaolin deposits (China clay) for whiter bodies
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Processed regolith fines plus flux additives once characterised
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Levigation to remove rocks; wedging; possibly ageing in anaerobic pits

rawMaterials.name: Temper
rawMaterials.purpose: Controls shrinkage and thermal shock resistance
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Crushed sintered regolith as grog analogue

rawMaterials.name: Fuel
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Charcoal preferred for high-fire with fewer ash incidents

buildSteps.title: Prospect and test clay
buildSteps.description: Roll coils and bend — good plasticity without crumbling indicates workable clay. Fire test briquettes through a stepped series to learn maturity temperature.
buildSteps.warningNote: Some clays contain soluble salts that bloom as efflorescence — note for food use

buildSteps.title: Prepare body
buildSteps.description: Levigate or dry process to remove organics if needed. Add temper at 10–30% by mass typical; wedge thoroughly.
buildSteps.warningNote: Air bubbles in thick walls explode — compress corners

buildSteps.title: Form and finish leather hard
buildSteps.description: Build by coil, press into moulds, or throw on a wheel if available. Trim feet and join handles with slip scoring.
buildSteps.warningNote: Join surfaces must be scored and slipped — butt joints fail

buildSteps.title: Controlled dry
buildSteps.description: Cover loosely to equalise moisture; invert bowls when rims stiffen. Target slow loss through critical shrinkage zone.
buildSteps.warningNote: Sun drying thin rims against thick bases causes S-cracks

buildSteps.title: Bisque fire
buildSteps.description: Slow ramp through water smoking zone; peak around 900–1000 °C for earthenware bisque depending on body. Cool closed to prevent thermal shock.
buildSteps.warningNote: Never open a hot kiln — radiant heat injures eyes and skin

buildSteps.title: Glaze and high fire (if used)
buildSteps.description: Apply glaze by dip or brush; wipe feet clean. Fire to glaze maturity; vent kiln appropriately for desired atmosphere.
buildSteps.warningNote: Lead-bearing folk glazes are toxic — avoid without modern safety

history.event: Earliest known fired ceramic figurines precede utilitarian pottery in some sequences — fire applied to clay artistically.
Vessel pottery spreads with sedentism and grain storage.
Kilns reach temperatures enabling proto-porcelain in some East Asian traditions.

impact:
Pottery changed diet and demography — storable cooked calories and safer water. Bricks changed architecture — modular, fireproof, locally manufacturable. Refractories made metal furnaces and glass tanks durable enough to be economic.

Ceramics are still everywhere: insulators, catalyst supports, bioceramics, armour. The pedagogical point is universal: earth plus heat plus time yields a material phase change civilisation depends on.
